PiggyVest vs Cowrywise. I have written about this before but this time I have checked their features and will like to settle this once and for all, PiggyVest was built for people who need their money working harder than they do. With Investify returning up to 35% per annum across real estate, agriculture, and commercial papers, and SafeLock paying your interest upfront before the lock period even ends, PiggyVest is unapologetically about maximising every naira. Add HouseMoney for rent, dollar savings, and automated deductions that won't let you cheat yourself, and you have a platform designed to make excuses impossible. Cowrywise, on the other hand, is for people who think beyond the next quarter. It sits on top of 20+ mutual funds from Nigeria's heaviest fund managers like Stanbic IBTC, ARM and United Capital, and actually builds you a portfolio. It hedges your wealth against the naira with dollar investments, runs a full Halal savings mode for users who refuse to compromise their faith for profit, and forces you to stay the course with strict no-early-withdrawal rules. That is not a bug. That is the entire point. PiggyVest wins the short game. Cowrywise wins the long game. The people arguing online about which is better are the same people who will still be broke in five years because they spent time debating instead of doing both. PiggyVest for the grind. Cowrywise for the vision.

Today, I just randomly remembered the time I was strongly advised against getting a car because it would make me ‘less desirable to men’. So, I had finally reached my target savings to get a car after a year of setting up my piggyvest account. Then, I recalled that a mommy in church had given me a lift one time and she recently got the car too. I don’t know much about cars but I really loved how hers looked on the inside and outside. Luckily for me, I was privy to the cost of the car purchase and it worked perfectly with my budget. Added to this, the mommy had mentioned in passing that she had a trusted mechanic who had accompanied her for the car purchase. In light of all these, she seemed like the perfect person to ask for car purchase advice and directions. You know, to link me up with her dealer, and then maybe let her mechanic tag along with me for a test drive and everything. Oh, how wrong I was! That was how, with Jesus joy in my heart and 6M in my virtual wallet, I cornered mommy after church service to seek counsel. Men and brethren, counsel, I got! But from a very different approach. Church mommy sat me down, and she began preaching to me. “Why do you want a car?” “Who would marry a single woman who is already driving around town without caution?” And by driving around town, she meant me driving myself to work and church. Also, I didn’t realise that single women driving around town was reckless behaviour. “How will men see you? You want to drive men away?” She continued. I sat through her sermon, while I silently chided myself for coming to her. Na me mess up. I don’t know what I was thinking or how I forgot this was the same woman who, if we were discussing and I mistakenly let it slip that I used a washing machine for my clothes or that I give my clothes to the laundry man, would hush me, advising me not to wash my dirty linen outside. That day, I listened to her attentively, thanked her for her ‘motherly’ advice, then proceeded to buy the car. The following Sunday, I made sure to park right beside hers. The shock on her face gave me pure satisfaction. It’s been two years and this mommy still believes that the car is the reason for my ‘singleness’. What mommy has failed to understand is that I do not desire to attract a man who is intimidated by something as ordinary as a car. A second-hand car for that matter. Mtchew. Sadly, I have come to realise that some of our older mommies in church don’t truly understand the concept of compatibility, especially in the area of logic and core values. Now, why would I want a man, who is intimidated by my career growth, by my success? Why should I choose mediocrity to keep a man? If having a car is the reason a man doesn’t pursue me, then he is clearly not my type. And honestly, it’s good riddance! Mind you, I'm not one to toot my own horn. However, for the context of the story, I have to add that I am not a materialistic person. I have never been driven by material possessions. I have never been moved by what a man has or does not have. And even though I do have a car, I don't mind if he doesn't have a car. What really matters to me are three non-negotiable things. One, he must be a lover of God, a genuine lover of God. Secondly, he must have something upstairs. He must be someone who thinks, someone who can hold conversations, someone who has a mind that can process thoughts, yes? And thirdly, he must be self-driven, who doesn't need to be motivated, someone who will push me as well to my dreams, someone who has a dream burning in his heart. It has never mattered if he has nothing, because the above, in my opinion, is everything. It’s been two years, and I am still single but no, I don’t believe it’s because I have a car or am driving recklessly around town. #Fiction

Stills from the third edition of our annual Finance Roundtable, where we presented the Piggyvest Savings Report 2025, and held some interesting conversations led by some of the most brilliant minds in the industry on financial and economic growth 💙 The Piggyvest savings report 2025 is live now. Find out what the data says here:

Every year since 2023, we’ve asked one question: How are Nigerians really doing with money? In 2025, we went deeper, deploying over 90 data collectors to reach more than 25,000 Nigerians across rural and urban communities in all six geopolitical zones. This year, beyond tracking income and spending, we wanted to understand something more honest: Are Nigerians actually moving forward… or just surviving? The Piggyvest Savings Report 2025 is here. Read it. Share it. Let's talk about it! 🚀 👉🏾:
A new PiggyVest report shows 30% of Nigerians earn under ₦100,000 monthly, while rising inflation and weak purchasing power leave most unsure their income can cover basic living costs.

One in 20 Nigerians now earns above ₦1 million ($723.26) monthly, according to data from savings platform Piggyvest’s new savings report. The share of those earning ₦1 million ($723.26) or more monthly rose to 5% in 2025, from 2% in 2024. The report, which surveyed over 25,000 Nigerians, shows that while income levels are improving across several brackets, inflation, especially on food, is widening the gap between earning more and living better. This reflects a broader economic reality in Nigeria, where income growth is struggling to keep pace with inflation and currency weakness. As prices rise faster than wages, higher earnings are doing little to improve living standards, leaving many households financially stretched despite nominal gains.

Let's tell ourselves the truth. This stat is misleading. PiggyVest users are not a perfect representation of all Nigerian adults. I don't even use them. Also their customer base is lean compared to the population of Nigeria: They just have digitally aware users, younger demographics, some salary earners and side hustlers. Using a platform-specific insight as a “national truth,” is a misinterpretation of fact.

According to the 2025 Piggyvest Savings Report, nearly 3 in 5 Nigerians earn less than ₦100,000 a month or nothing at all. For many people, that’s not for lack of effort but the reality of a tough economy. 🧵👇🏾

A report by Piggyvest reveals that nearly 60% of Nigerians either earn below ₦100,000 monthly or have no income at all, highlighting deepening income pressures across the country. Despite nominal wage increases, rising inflation has significantly eroded purchasing power, leaving many households struggling to meet basic needs. Until you are able to scale through survival mode forget about having a savings or building investment portfolio. Or else you will just be putting your money in Piggyvest or Cowrywise and going back to collect it. The only way to save well and build investment portfolio is when you have growing your income flow to a reasonable extent. So instead of allowing peer pressure push you into a lifestyle that you can't keep up with make effort to upskill or set up extra income stream. The more you earn the easier it is to save. So you goal should be to jack up your earnings such that your basic life needs are sorted then you can push the remaining into investment and all. Na person way chop belleful dey invest but the investment mindset is a must for anyone that wants to make something tangible out of life. PiggyVest vs Afrinvest - They are not the same thing PiggyVest and Afrinvest are often mentioned together, but they serve very different purposes in the financial ecosystem. PiggyVest is primarily a savings and retail investment platform designed to help individuals build financial discipline. It focuses on automated savings, fixed-income opportunities, and simple goal-based investing. The platform aggregates investment opportunities and partners with licensed institutions, making it accessible for beginners and salary earners who want structure without deep market knowledge. Afrinvest, on the other hand, is a full-service investment and financial advisory firm regulated by the Securities and Exchange Commission. It operates within the capital markets, offering services such as stock trading, treasury bills, bonds, mutual funds, and portfolio management. Afrinvest is positioned more as an investment house than a savings platform, with strong research capabilities and institutional-grade services. In practical terms, PiggyVest helps you build and preserve capital through disciplined saving and low-to-moderate risk investments. Afrinvest helps you deploy capital into the market, where returns are driven by asset performance, strategy, and economic conditions. The difference is not about which is better, it is about where you are in your financial journey. PiggyVest fits early-stage investors who want simplicity, consistency, and structure. Afrinvest fits investors who want deeper exposure to markets, asset allocation, and professional management. The smartest approach is understanding that both can complement each other. One helps you accumulate capital, the other helps you grow and position it. Financial tools should match your stage, not trends.

Anyways, here is a step by step guide.. -Create a system by putting your money in percentages, 50-30-20 rule is a guide, 50% for your essentials, 30 percent saving and investment and 20% for dorime, tweak it however way works best for you. -Build a 6 months emergency fund, this can be in your piggyvest where it’s accessible to you max 48hours -Determine your risk appetite and find an investment instrument that suits your risk, low risk, money market mutual fund, high risk consider stocks investing, both Nigeria and foreign. -Be consistent with them and you will be fine.

The content marketing team at piggyvest worked very hard to put together last year’s savings report. This research was carried out across study states are Bauchi, Cross River, Ebonyi, Gombe, Imo, Kano, Kebbi, Kogi, Ondo, Oyo, Plateau, and Rivers. Our quantitative research relied solely on in-person field data collection across the 12 selected states. Using a multi-stage sampling strategy, field researchers surveyed respondents from urban, peri-urban, and rural settlements to ensure broad representation. This nationwide exercise reached over 26,000 Nigerians, offering a robust and geographically diverse sample across income groups and occupations. To complement the quantitative findings, qualitative data were gathered through Focus Group Discussions (FGDs) and Key Informant Interviews (Klls). Participants included salary earners, small business owners, students, local community members, sector experts The qualitative component provided deeper insight into how Nigerians navigate income volatility, saving behaviour, debt, and financial stress.
